name: गुणत्रयविभागयोग
teaching: All of nature is woven of three strands: sattva binds with clarity and the pride of knowing, rajas with craving and restlessness, tamas with heaviness and sleep. Even goodness is a golden chain. Freedom is not perfecting the weave but seeing the weave — knowing the guṇas move among the guṇas while the witness stands apart. Three strands weave every mood, meal, and motive — naming them is half the freedom. The exit is not war against the weave but devotion to its Lord, who declares Himself the very foundation of Brahman.
questions: Which strand dominated your last twenty-four hours — clarity, agitation, or dullness?
How does your sattva bind you — what subtle pride rides on your discipline or insight?
The one beyond the guṇas 'neither hates their presence nor longs for their absence.' Can you let today's mood simply move through?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
परं भूयः प्रवक्ष्यामि ज्ञानानां ज्ञानमुत्तमम् ।
यज्ज्ञात्वा मुनयः सर्वे परां सिद्धिमितो गताः॥
verses.transliteration:
paraṃ bhūyaḥ pravakṣyāmi jñānānāṃ jñānamuttamam
yajjñātvā munayaḥ sarve parāṃ siddhimito gatāḥ
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: again I shall declare the supreme knowledge, best of all knowledges, knowing which all the sages have gone from here to the highest perfection.
verses.translation: I will again declare (to thee) that supreme knowledge, the best of all knowledge, having known which all the sages have gone to the supreme perfection after this life.

anvaya.meaning: the supreme, again, I shall declare
to the supreme perfection, from here, have gone

verses.explanation: Unprompted — no question from Arjuna this time — Krishna volunteers another summit: jñānānāṁ jñānam uttamam, the best of all knowings. Its credential is historical: every sage who ever left this place for perfection traveled by it. The subject will turn out to be the three strands every mind is woven from.
verses.connection: Opens the guna-analysis: ch13 mapped field and knower; ch14 maps the field's three threads.
verses.reflection: Receive the unprompted teaching today — wisdom that arrives before you knew to ask for it.
verses.background: Krishna volunteers a further depth: I shall declare again the supreme knowledge by which sages have gone from here to perfection — and frames all becoming as a cosmic conception: the great Brahman is My womb, in it I place the seed; from that comes the birth of all beings, whatever the womb, and I am the seed-giving father.

verses.devanagari:
इदं ज्ञानमुपाश्रित्य मम साधर्म्यमागताः ।
सर्गेऽपि नोपजायन्ते प्रलये न व्यथन्ति च॥
verses.transliteration:
idaṃ jñānamupāśritya mama sādharmyamāgatāḥ
sarge'pi nopajāyante pralaye na vyathanti ca
verses.essence: Taking refuge in this knowledge, having come to share my own nature, they are not born even at a new creation, nor troubled at the dissolution.
verses.translation: They who, having taken refuge in this knowledge, have attained to unity with Me, are neither born at the time of creation nor are they disturbed at the time of dissolution.

anvaya.meaning: having come to likeness with my nature
even at a creation, they are not born
nor at the dissolution do they tremble

verses.explanation: The stakes, stated cosmically: mama sādharmyam — likeness to his very nature, the strongest union-language outside chapter 11. Such knowers stand outside the universe's own heartbeat: when worlds are exhaled they are not re-embodied; when worlds are inhaled they do not flinch. Guna-knowledge, fully ripened, exempts one from the cosmic calendar itself.
verses.connection: Prices the knowledge of 14.1: beyond rebirth, beyond even the rebirth of universes.
verses.reflection: Aim high enough tonight: a knowing that stands calm even while worlds are exhaled and inhaled.
verses.background: Krishna volunteers a further depth: I shall declare again the supreme knowledge by which sages have gone from here to perfection — and frames all becoming as a cosmic conception: the great Brahman is My womb, in it I place the seed; from that comes the birth of all beings, whatever the womb, and I am the seed-giving father.

verses.devanagari:
मम योनिर्महद् ब्रह्म तस्मिन्गर्भं दधाम्यहम् ।
सम्भवः सर्वभूतानां ततो भवति भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
mama yonirmahad brahma tasmingarbhaṃ dadhāmyaham
sambhavaḥ sarvabhūtānāṃ tato bhavati bhārata
verses.essence: My womb is the great Brahman; in that I place the seed. From that, Bharata, comes the origin of all beings.
verses.translation: My womb is the great Brahma; in that I place the germ; thence, O Arjuna, is the birth of all beings.

anvaya.meaning: my womb is the great Brahman (prakriti)
in that I place the embryo

verses.explanation: Creation as conception: mahad brahma — vast nature herself — as womb, and Krishna as the one who plants the garbha, the seed of consciousness. The metaphor honors both parents: matter is not dead stuff awaiting a command but the great mother; spirit is not a distant architect but the intimate father. Every being is their child.
verses.connection: The cosmology behind 13.27's field-knower union: the marriage now imaged as parenthood.
verses.schools_note: 'Mama yonir mahad brahma' — My womb is the great Brahman: here 'brahma' startles by meaning prakriti, and the commentators all pause to say so. Shankara: maya as the Lord's generative power; Ramanuja: the great element of matter, His mode; the Gaudiyas: the brahmajyoti or material totality receiving the seed of souls. The shared image is the point — creation as conception, both parents divine.
verses.reflection: Honor both your parents in everything today: matter the great womb, spirit the seed.
verses.background: Krishna volunteers a further depth: I shall declare again the supreme knowledge by which sages have gone from here to perfection — and frames all becoming as a cosmic conception: the great Brahman is My womb, in it I place the seed; from that comes the birth of all beings, whatever the womb, and I am the seed-giving father.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वयोनिषु कौन्तेय मूर्तयः सम्भवन्ति याः ।
तासां ब्रह्म महद्योनिरहं बीजप्रदः पिता॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvayoniṣu kaunteya mūrtayaḥ sambhavanti yāḥ
tāsāṃ brahma mahadyonirahaṃ bījapradaḥ pitā
verses.essence: Whatever forms arise in all wombs, Kaunteya — the great Brahman is their womb, and I am the seed-giving father.
verses.translation: Whatever forms are produced, O Arjuna, in any womb whatsoever, the great Brahma is their womb and I am the seed-giving father.

anvaya.meaning: of them the great Brahman is the womb

verses.explanation: The claim universalized: sarva-yoniṣu, *all* wombs — moth, mongoose, monarch, god. One mother, one father, behind every birth in every species; the family of beings is literal, not poetic. For Arjuna, facing 'enemies', the genealogy is pointed: everyone on that field has the same two parents he does.
verses.connection: Extends 14.3 to every species — the metaphysical ground of universal kinship.
verses.reflection: Greet one creature of another species today as family — same mother, same father, truly.
verses.background: Krishna volunteers a further depth: I shall declare again the supreme knowledge by which sages have gone from here to perfection — and frames all becoming as a cosmic conception: the great Brahman is My womb, in it I place the seed; from that comes the birth of all beings, whatever the womb, and I am the seed-giving father.

verses.devanagari:
सत्त्वं रजस्तम इति गुणाः प्रकृतिसम्भवाः ।
निबध्नन्ति महाबाहो देहे देहिनमव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sattvaṃ rajastama iti guṇāḥ prakṛtisambhavāḥ
nibadhnanti mahābāho dehe dehinamavyayam
verses.essence: Sattva, rajas, tamas — these gunas, born of prakriti, bind the imperishable embodied one in the body, Mahabaho.
verses.translation: Purity, passion and inertia these alities, O Arjuna, born of Nature, bind fast in the body, the embodied, the indestructible.

anvaya.meaning: in the body, the embodied one, imperishable

verses.explanation: The chapter's three protagonists enter: luminous sattva, restless rajas, heavy tamas — guna literally meaning 'strand' or 'rope.' The paradox is stated whole: they bind the avyayam, the *unbindable*. How does rope hold what cannot be held? By persuasion, the next verses show — each strand binds with a different bait.
verses.connection: Names the three ropes; 14.6–8 will examine each knot.
verses.reflection: Learn your three ropes by name today: sattva, rajas, tamas — you cannot loosen what you cannot name.
verses.background: The three strands are introduced by name and grip: sattva, rajas, tamas, born of prakriti, binding the imperishable dweller in the body — sattva by attachment to happiness and knowledge, rajas by thirst and attachment to action, tamas by negligence, sloth, and sleep, veiling knowledge.

verses.devanagari:
तत्र सत्त्वं निर्मलत्वात्प्रकाशकमनामयम् ।
सुखसङ्गेन बध्नाति ज्ञानसङ्गेन चानघ॥
verses.transliteration:
tatra sattvaṃ nirmalatvātprakāśakamanāmayam
sukhasaṅgena badhnāti jñānasaṅgena cānagha
verses.essence: Of these, sattva — stainless, luminous, free of affliction — binds by attachment to happiness and by attachment to knowledge, sinless one.
verses.translation: Of these, Sattva, which from its stainlessness is luminous and healthy, binds by attachment to happiness and by attachment to knowledge, O sinless one.

anvaya.meaning: and by attachment to knowledge, O sinless one

verses.explanation: The subtlest trap first: sattva, everything one would want — clear, bright, healthy — and still a rope. Its baits are the finest available: sukha-saṅga, the clinging to feeling good, and jñāna-saṅga, the clinging to *being the one who knows*. A golden chain is the teaching's classic gloss: beautiful, comfortable, and still a chain.
verses.connection: The first knot examined — and the chapter's hardest lesson: even the best quality binds.
verses.reflection: Inspect even your virtues tonight: where has feeling-good, or being-the-one-who-knows, become a chain?
verses.background: The three strands are introduced by name and grip: sattva, rajas, tamas, born of prakriti, binding the imperishable dweller in the body — sattva by attachment to happiness and knowledge, rajas by thirst and attachment to action, tamas by negligence, sloth, and sleep, veiling knowledge.

verses.devanagari:
रजो रागात्मकं विद्धि तृष्णासङ्गसमुद्भवम् ।
तन्निबध्नाति कौन्तेय कर्मसङ्गेन देहिनम्॥
verses.transliteration:
rajo rāgātmakaṃ viddhi tṛṣṇāsaṅgasamudbhavam
tannibadhnāti kaunteya karmasaṅgena dehinam
verses.essence: Know rajas to be of the nature of passion, the source of craving and clinging; it binds the embodied one, Kaunteya, by attachment to action.
verses.translation: Know thou Rajas to be of the nature of passion, the source of thirst (for sensual enjoyment) and attachment; it binds fast, O Arjuna, the embodied one by attachment to action.

anvaya.meaning: know rajas as having the nature of raga (passion)
the source of craving and clinging
by attachment to action, the embodied one

verses.explanation: The middle strand: rajas, pure momentum — tṛṣṇā, the thirst that drinking only sharpens, and saṅga, the grip. Its rope is karma-saṅga: addiction not to any particular outcome but to *doing itself*, the itch that makes stillness feel like dying. Most of what the world calls ambition is this strand, holding the leash.
verses.connection: The second knot: the restless rope that keeps the wheel of 3.5 spinning.
verses.reflection: Catch the thirst that drinking sharpens once today — and skip one round of feeding it.
verses.background: The three strands are introduced by name and grip: sattva, rajas, tamas, born of prakriti, binding the imperishable dweller in the body — sattva by attachment to happiness and knowledge, rajas by thirst and attachment to action, tamas by negligence, sloth, and sleep, veiling knowledge.

verses.devanagari:
तमस्त्वज्ञानजं विद्धि मोहनं सर्वदेहिनाम् ।
प्रमादालस्यनिद्राभिस्तन्निबध्नाति भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
tamastvajñānajaṃ viddhi mohanaṃ sarvadehinām
pramādālasyanidrābhistannibadhnāti bhārata
verses.essence: But know tamas as born of ignorance, deluding all embodied beings; it binds, Bharata, with negligence, laziness and sleep.
verses.translation: But know thou Tamas to be born of ignorance, deluding all embodied beings; it binds fast, O Arjuna, by heedlessness, indolence and sleep.

anvaya.meaning: but tamas, know as born of ignorance
the deluder of all embodied beings

verses.explanation: The heaviest strand: tamas, darkness as a force — not the absence of energy but an active mohanam, a deluding fog. Its three ropes need no bait at all: pramāda, the slow leak of carelessness; ālasya, the body's refusal; nidrā, sleep beyond the body's need. Where rajas binds by motion, tamas binds by weight.
verses.connection: The third knot completes the set — three ropes, three entirely different grips.
verses.reflection: Name tamas honestly where you find it today: the fog, the heaviness, the not-caring — naming thins it.
verses.background: The three strands are introduced by name and grip: sattva, rajas, tamas, born of prakriti, binding the imperishable dweller in the body — sattva by attachment to happiness and knowledge, rajas by thirst and attachment to action, tamas by negligence, sloth, and sleep, veiling knowledge.

verses.devanagari:
सत्त्वं सुखे सञ्जयति रजः कर्मणि भारत ।
ज्ञानमावृत्य तु तमः प्रमादे सञ्जयत्युत॥
verses.transliteration:
sattvaṃ sukhe sañjayati rajaḥ karmaṇi bhārata
jñānamāvṛtya tu tamaḥ pramāde sañjayatyuta
verses.essence: Sattva attaches one to happiness, rajas to action, Bharata; but tamas, veiling knowledge, attaches one to negligence.
verses.translation: Sattva attaches to happiness, Rajas to action, O Arjuna, while Tamas verily shrouding knowledge attaches to heedlessness.
verses.explanation: The summary table, one verb three ways: sañjayati, it attaches. Sattva glues you to feeling-good, rajas to doing, tamas — after first pulling the blind down over knowing — to not-caring. Notice the diagnostic gift: identify what you cannot stop reaching for, and you have identified which strand is currently holding the pen.
verses.connection: Compresses 14.6–8 into a field guide: three attachments, three rulers.
verses.reflection: Run the diagnostic at noon: what can I not stop reaching for right now? That names the ruler.
verses.background: The three strands are introduced by name and grip: sattva, rajas, tamas, born of prakriti, binding the imperishable dweller in the body — sattva by attachment to happiness and knowledge, rajas by thirst and attachment to action, tamas by negligence, sloth, and sleep, veiling knowledge.

verses.devanagari:
रजस्तमश्चाभिभूय सत्त्वं भवति भारत ।
रजः सत्त्वं तमश्चैव तमः सत्त्वं रजस्तथा॥
verses.transliteration:
rajastamaścābhibhūya sattvaṃ bhavati bhārata
rajaḥ sattvaṃ tamaścaiva tamaḥ sattvaṃ rajastathā
verses.essence: Sattva prevails, Bharata, overpowering rajas and tamas; rajas, overpowering sattva and tamas; and tamas, overpowering sattva and rajas.
verses.translation: Now Sattva arises (prevails), O Arjuna, having overpowered Rajas and Tamas; nor Rajas, having overpowered Sattva and Tamas; and now Tamas, having overpowered Sattva and Rajas.

anvaya.meaning: sattva comes to be, O Bharata

verses.explanation: The inner weather report: the three are never absent, only in shifting majority — each rises abhibhūya, by *overpowering* the other two. A single day cycles through all three governments: the clear morning, the driven afternoon, the leaden evening. The self watches a parliament it did not elect form and fall, hour by hour.
verses.connection: The dynamics: 14.6–9's strands as rivals in perpetual coup — observable in any afternoon.
verses.reflection: Watch one full cycle today — clear morning, driven afternoon, heavy evening — as governments changing.
verses.background: The strands are shown contending for government: now sattva prevails over rajas and tamas, now rajas, now tamas — and the field signs of each ruler are given: light at all the body's gates when sattva reigns; greed, restlessness, and craving under rajas; darkness, inertia, and delusion under tamas.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वद्वारेषु देहेऽस्मिन्प्रकाश उपजायते ।
ज्ञानं यदा तदा विद्याद्विवृद्धं सत्त्वमित्युत॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvadvāreṣu dehe'sminprakāśa upajāyate
jñānaṃ yadā tadā vidyādvivṛddhaṃ sattvamityuta
verses.essence: When through all the gates of this body the light of understanding shines forth — then one may know that sattva has increased.
verses.translation: When through every gate (sense) in this body, the wisdom-light shines, then it may be known that Sattva is predominant.

anvaya.meaning: in all the gates of this body
knowledge — when (this is so), then one should know

verses.explanation: The diagnostics begin — how to read which strand is ruling. Sattva's sign: prakāśa at sarva-dvāreṣu, light at *all* the gates — the nine doors of 5.13 now windows. Perception crisp, words clean, the world arriving in focus. When everything simply seems clearer, that is not the world improving; that is sattva ascendant.
verses.connection: First entry in the field-manual (14.11–13): sattva announced by clarity at the senses.
verses.reflection: Notice an hour when all the gates are lit — perception crisp, words clean — and thank sattva by name.
verses.background: The strands are shown contending for government: now sattva prevails over rajas and tamas, now rajas, now tamas — and the field signs of each ruler are given: light at all the body's gates when sattva reigns; greed, restlessness, and craving under rajas; darkness, inertia, and delusion under tamas.

verses.devanagari:
लोभः प्रवृत्तिरारम्भः कर्मणामशमः स्पृहा ।
रजस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे भरतर्षभ॥
verses.transliteration:
lobhaḥ pravṛttirārambhaḥ karmaṇāmaśamaḥ spṛhā
rajasyetāni jāyante vivṛddhe bharatarṣabha
verses.essence: Greed, busyness, the undertaking of works, restlessness, craving — these arise, best of Bharatas, when rajas has increased.
verses.translation: Greed, activity, the undertaking of actions, restlessness, longing these arise when Rajas is predominant, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: has increased, O bull of the Bharatas

verses.explanation: Rajas's five tells, each recognizable from the inside: lobha, the more-hunger; pravṛtti, the compulsion to be in motion; ārambha, the launching of project after project; aśama, the inability to sit; spṛhā, the reflexive reaching. A culture can be read with this verse as easily as a mood — and modernity reads rajasic on every line.
verses.connection: Second entry: rajas announced not by feeling but by the calendar filling itself.
verses.reflection: Read your calendar as a symptom today: greed, launching, restlessness — rajas writes in appointments.
verses.background: The strands are shown contending for government: now sattva prevails over rajas and tamas, now rajas, now tamas — and the field signs of each ruler are given: light at all the body's gates when sattva reigns; greed, restlessness, and craving under rajas; darkness, inertia, and delusion under tamas.

verses.devanagari:
अप्रकाशोऽप्रवृत्तिश्च प्रमादो मोह एव च ।
तमस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे कुरुनन्दन॥
verses.transliteration:
aprakāśo'pravṛttiśca pramādo moha eva ca
tamasyetāni jāyante vivṛddhe kurunandana
verses.essence: Darkness, inertia, negligence, and delusion — these arise, Kurunandana, when tamas has increased.
verses.translation: Darkness, inertness, heedlessness and delusion these arise when Tamas is predominant, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: has increased, O joy of the Kurus

verses.explanation: Tamas diagnosed by negation: aprakāśa, the lights dimmed; apravṛtti, the engine that will not turn; then pramāda and moha, the slide from not-doing into not-seeing-why-it-matters. The danger is its self-concealment — sattva and rajas can notice themselves; tamas, by definition, has trouble noticing anything.
verses.connection: Third entry completes the manual — and 14.14–15 asks: which strand will you die in?
verses.reflection: Be alert for the strand that hides itself: when nothing seems to matter, that is tamas, mattering.
verses.background: The strands are shown contending for government: now sattva prevails over rajas and tamas, now rajas, now tamas — and the field signs of each ruler are given: light at all the body's gates when sattva reigns; greed, restlessness, and craving under rajas; darkness, inertia, and delusion under tamas.

verses.devanagari:
यदा सत्त्वे प्रवृद्धे तु प्रलयं याति देहभृत् ।
तदोत्तमविदां लोकानमलान्प्रतिपद्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā sattve pravṛddhe tu pralayaṃ yāti dehabhṛt
tadottamavidāṃ lokānamalānpratipadyate
verses.essence: When the embodied one meets dissolution while sattva prevails, then one attains the stainless worlds of those who know the highest.
verses.translation: If the embodied one meets with death when Sattva is predominant, then he attains to the spotless worlds of the knowers of the Highest.

anvaya.meaning: then, to the worlds of the knowers of the highest

verses.explanation: The strands follow past the body: die on a sattva tide, and the momentum carries to amalān lokān, stainless worlds, the company of the wise. Not a judgment passed from outside — just physics: the quality of consciousness at the moment of release is a trajectory, and trajectories continue. Chapter 8's final-thought doctrine, restated in guna terms.
verses.connection: Begins the death-accounting (14.14–15): the ruling strand as the soul's launch angle.
verses.reflection: Practice dying well by living well today: the tide you cultivate is the tide that carries.
verses.background: The strands follow the soul to the door of death and past it: dying in sattva, one reaches the pure worlds of the knowers; in rajas, birth among the action-bound; in tamas, the wombs of the deluded. Fruit follows seed — purity, pain, ignorance — and the directions: upward go the sattvic, the rajasic stand midway, downward the tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
रजसि प्रलयं गत्वा कर्मसङ्गिषु जायते ।
तथा प्रलीनस्तमसि मूढयोनिषु जायते॥
verses.transliteration:
rajasi pralayaṃ gatvā karmasaṅgiṣu jāyate
tathā pralīnastamasi mūḍhayoniṣu jāyate
verses.essence: Meeting dissolution in rajas, one is born among those attached to action; and dissolved in tamas, one is born in the wombs of the deluded.
verses.translation: Meeting death in Rajas, he is born among those who are attached to action; and dying in Tamas, he is born in the womb of the senseless.

anvaya.meaning: having gone to dissolution in rajas
one is born among those attached to action
one is born in deluded wombs

verses.explanation: The other two launch angles: a rajasic death lands among karma-saṅgis — the driven, the striving, another lifetime on the wheel of doing; a tamasic one sinks toward mūḍha-yonis, births where delusion is the native air. No tribunal anywhere in the verse: like settles toward like, water finding its level across lifetimes.
verses.connection: Completes 14.14: three deaths, three gravities — motive enough for the cultivation 14.16–18 maps.
verses.reflection: Let like-settles-toward-like motivate one upgrade today — no tribunal, just trajectory.
verses.background: The strands follow the soul to the door of death and past it: dying in sattva, one reaches the pure worlds of the knowers; in rajas, birth among the action-bound; in tamas, the wombs of the deluded. Fruit follows seed — purity, pain, ignorance — and the directions: upward go the sattvic, the rajasic stand midway, downward the tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
कर्मणः सुकृतस्याहुः सात्त्विकं निर्मलं फलम् ।
रजसस्तु फलं दुःखमज्ञानं तमसः फलम्॥
verses.transliteration:
karmaṇaḥ sukṛtasyāhuḥ sāttvikaṃ nirmalaṃ phalam
rajasastu phalaṃ duḥkhamajñānaṃ tamasaḥ phalam
verses.essence: Of good action, they say, the fruit is sattvic and stainless; the fruit of rajas is pain; and ignorance is the fruit of tamas.
verses.translation: The fruit of good action, they say, is Sattvic and pure, verily the fruit of Rajas is pain, and ignorance is the fruit of Tamas.

anvaya.meaning: of action well done, they say
but of rajas, the fruit is pain
ignorance is the fruit of tamas

verses.explanation: The harvest table: sattvic action ripens clean; rajasic action — whatever it wins — ripens as duḥkha, since craving's victories arrive pre-loaded with the next craving; tamasic action ripens as deeper ajñāna, ignorance compounding like a debt. Each strand pays its workers in its own currency, and the currency is more of itself.
verses.connection: The fruit-ledger that 14.17 traces back to its psychological roots.
verses.reflection: Check the harvest, not the seed packet, tonight: what did today's actions actually ripen into?
verses.background: The strands follow the soul to the door of death and past it: dying in sattva, one reaches the pure worlds of the knowers; in rajas, birth among the action-bound; in tamas, the wombs of the deluded. Fruit follows seed — purity, pain, ignorance — and the directions: upward go the sattvic, the rajasic stand midway, downward the tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
सत्त्वात्सञ्जायते ज्ञानं रजसो लोभ एव च ।
प्रमादमोहौ तमसो भवतोऽज्ञानमेव च॥
verses.transliteration:
sattvātsañjāyate jñānaṃ rajaso lobha eva ca
pramādamohau tamaso bhavato'jñānameva ca
verses.essence: From sattva arises knowledge, and from rajas, greed; from tamas arise negligence and delusion — and ignorance too.
verses.translation: From Sattva arises knowledge, and greed from Rajas; heedlessness and delusion arise from Tamas, and also ignorance.
verses.explanation: The strands as factories, each with one product line: sattva manufactures jñāna, knowing; rajas manufactures lobha, the wanting of more; tamas runs three shifts — negligence, delusion, ignorance. The practical inference is the chapter's quiet gift: to get more knowledge, do not chase knowledge; cultivate the strand that produces it.
verses.connection: The production chain behind 14.16's fruits — quality of being precedes quality of mind.
verses.reflection: Want more knowledge? Do not chase it today — cultivate the strand that manufactures it.
verses.background: The strands follow the soul to the door of death and past it: dying in sattva, one reaches the pure worlds of the knowers; in rajas, birth among the action-bound; in tamas, the wombs of the deluded. Fruit follows seed — purity, pain, ignorance — and the directions: upward go the sattvic, the rajasic stand midway, downward the tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
ऊर्ध्वं गच्छन्ति सत्त्वस्था मध्ये तिष्ठन्ति राजसाः ।
जघन्यगुणवृत्तिस्था अधो गच्छन्ति तामसाः॥
verses.transliteration:
ūrdhvaṃ gacchanti sattvasthā madhye tiṣṭhanti rājasāḥ
jaghanyaguṇavṛttisthā adho gacchanti tāmasāḥ
verses.essence: Those established in sattva go upward; the rajasic remain in the middle; the tamasic, abiding in the lowest tendency, go downward.
verses.translation: Those who are seated in Sattva go upwards; the Rajasic dwell in the middle; and the Tamasic, abiding in the function of the lowest Guna, go downwards.

anvaya.meaning: upward go those established in sattva
in the middle stand the rajasic
abiding in the function of the lowest guna

verses.explanation: The whole cosmology as a single elevator: ūrdhvam, madhye, adhaḥ — up, level, down. The strands are not moral categories but densities, and beings sort by buoyancy. Yet the verse describes a treadmill even at its top: 'upward' is still *within* the three-strand machine. The next verse opens the door out of the building entirely.
verses.connection: The vertical map of the guna-world — drawn so 14.19 can point beyond all three floors.
verses.reflection: Locate your floor honestly this evening — rising, level, or sinking — then look for the stairwell out.
verses.background: The strands follow the soul to the door of death and past it: dying in sattva, one reaches the pure worlds of the knowers; in rajas, birth among the action-bound; in tamas, the wombs of the deluded. Fruit follows seed — purity, pain, ignorance — and the directions: upward go the sattvic, the rajasic stand midway, downward the tamasic.

verses.devanagari:
नान्यं गुणेभ्यः कर्तारं यदा द्रष्टानुपश्यति ।
गुणेभ्यश्च परं वेत्ति मद्भावं सोऽधिगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
nānyaṃ guṇebhyaḥ kartāraṃ yadā draṣṭānupaśyati
guṇebhyaśca paraṃ vetti madbhāvaṃ so'dhigacchati
verses.essence: When the seer beholds no doer other than the gunas, and knows That which is beyond the gunas — one attains my being.
verses.translation: When the seer beholds no agent other than the Gunas and knows That which is higher than they, he attains to My Being.

anvaya.meaning: no doer other than the gunas
and knows what is beyond the gunas

verses.explanation: The exit, in two recognitions: first, nānyaṁ guṇebhyaḥ kartāram — all doing, every act ever performed, is strand acting on strand; the 'doer' was the gunas wearing a name. Second, the seer knows the param, what stands outside the weave. Draṣṭā, the watcher: the one faculty no strand can perform. Watch the weather long enough as weather, and you discover you are the sky. Nanyam gunebhyah kartaram — when the seer beholds no doer but the strands, and knows what stands beyond them: the commentaries call this the chapter's moksha-verse, the seeing that 3.27 planted and 13.30 watered now bearing final fruit. Mad-bhavam so 'dhigachchhati — and the destination is personal: he attains My being. Even the most analytic liberation in the Gita ends at a Someone.
verses.connection: The liberating insight: 3.27 and 13.30 fused — and crowned with arrival in his being.
verses.reflection: Say the freeing sentence over one strong mood today: no doer here but the strands — and watch from the sky.
verses.background: The chapter's verses of release: when the seer beholds no doer other than the strands and knows what is higher than the strands, he attains My being — transcending the three that birthed the body, freed from birth, death, old age, and pain, he tastes immortality.

verses.devanagari:
गुणानेतानतीत्य त्रीन्देही देहसमुद्भवान् ।
जन्ममृत्युजरादुःखैर्विमुक्तोऽमृतमश्नुते॥
verses.transliteration:
guṇānetānatītya trīndehī dehasamudbhavān
janmamṛtyujarāduḥkhairvimukto'mṛtamaśnute
verses.essence: Transcending these three gunas, which are the source of the body, the embodied one — released from birth, death, old age and sorrow — attains immortality.
verses.translation: The embodied one having crossed beyond these three Gunas out of which the body is evolved, is freed from birth, death, decay and pain, and attains to immortality.

anvaya.meaning: the embodied one — (the gunas) the source of the body
from birth, death, old age, sorrow

verses.explanation: The full prize: the gunas are deha-samudbhavān, the very loom the body is woven on — step past them and the whole production line of embodiment, with its fixed menu of birth-death-age-sorrow, loses its claim. Amṛtam aśnute: one *tastes* deathlessness, the same sensory verb as 13.13 — liberation as flavor, not theory.
verses.connection: States the stakes of 14.19's seeing — and provokes Arjuna's immediate, practical question.
verses.reflection: Taste the full stakes once: past the three strands lies freedom from the body's whole fixed menu.
verses.background: The chapter's verses of release: when the seer beholds no doer other than the strands and knows what is higher than the strands, he attains My being — transcending the three that birthed the body, freed from birth, death, old age, and pain, he tastes immortality.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
कैर्लिङ्गैस्त्रीन्गुणानेतानतीतो भवति प्रभो ।
किमाचारः कथं चैतांस्त्रीन्गुणानतिवर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
kairliṅgaistrīnguṇānetānatīto bhavati prabho
kimācāraḥ kathaṃ caitāṃstrīnguṇānativartate
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: by what marks is one known who has gone beyond these three gunas, O Lord? What is such a one's conduct? And how does one pass beyond these three gunas?
verses.translation: What are the marks of him who has transcended the three alities, O Lord? What is his conduct and how does he go beyond these three alities?

anvaya.meaning: by what marks, these three gunas
having transcended, is one, O Lord
of what conduct? and how, these
three gunas does one pass beyond

verses.explanation: Arjuna's recurring question — he asked it of the sthita-prajña in 2.54 — returns for the guṇātīta: how would I recognize one? how do they live? how is it done? The repetition is the Gita's pedagogy: every metaphysical summit must be brought down to gait and gesture, or it remains decoration.
verses.connection: Echoes 2.54 exactly: the demand for a walking, breathing portrait of 14.20's freed one.
verses.reflection: Ask Arjuna's practical question of every ideal you meet: how is it recognized, lived, and reached?
verses.background: Arjuna asks the practical question — by what marks is the one beyond the strands known, and how does he cross them? Krishna gives the gunatita portrait: hating nothing that comes, craving nothing that ceases, seated as one unconcerned while the strands turn, equal in clod, stone, and gold — and then the method that overturns the chapter's analytics: unswerving devotion to Me carries one across, for I am the very foundation of Brahman, of immortality, and of absolute bliss.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
प्रकाशं च प्रवृत्तिं च मोहमेव च पाण्डव ।
न द्वेष्टि सम्प्रवृत्तानि न निवृत्तानि काङ्क्षति॥
verses.transliteration:
prakāśaṃ ca pravṛttiṃ ca mohameva ca pāṇḍava
na dveṣṭi sampravṛttāni na nivṛttāni kāṅkṣati
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: one who does not hate illumination, activity and delusion when they arise, Pandava, nor longs for them when they cease —
verses.translation: When light, activity and delusion are present, he hates them not, nor does he long for them when they are absent.

anvaya.meaning: does not hate when they have arisen
nor longs for them when ceased

verses.explanation: The answer's first surprise: the transcender does not hate even *tamas*. Prakāśa, pravṛtti, moha — sattva's light, rajas's drive, tamas's fog — arrive and depart like weather, and the guṇātīta neither resents the storm nor pines for the sun. Fighting a guna is done *with* a guna; only the unresisting watcher stands outside the game.
verses.connection: Begins the gunatita-portrait (14.22–25): transcendence as the end of the inner argument with one's own states.
verses.reflection: Practice non-hatred of your own states today — even the fog. Fighting a guna is done with a guna.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the practical question — by what marks is the one beyond the strands known, and how does he cross them? Krishna gives the gunatita portrait: hating nothing that comes, craving nothing that ceases, seated as one unconcerned while the strands turn, equal in clod, stone, and gold — and then the method that overturns the chapter's analytics: unswerving devotion to Me carries one across, for I am the very foundation of Brahman, of immortality, and of absolute bliss.

verses.devanagari:
उदासीनवदासीनो गुणैर्यो न विचाल्यते ।
गुणा वर्तन्त इत्येवं योऽवतिष्ठति नेङ्गते॥
verses.transliteration:
udāsīnavadāsīno guṇairyo na vicālyate
guṇā vartanta ityevaṃ yo'vatiṣṭhati neṅgate
verses.essence: Seated as one indifferent, unshaken by the gunas; 'the gunas are operating' — knowing thus, one stands firm and does not waver.
verses.translation: He who, seated like one unconcerned, is not moved by the alities, and who, knowing that the alities are active, is self-centred and moves not.

anvaya.meaning: who is not shaken by the gunas
who stands firm, and does not stir

verses.explanation: The inner posture, with its mantra included: guṇā vartanta iti — 'strands are turning.' Mood arrives: strands turning. Energy crashes: strands turning. The attribution does all the work, the same move as 3.28, now a settled stance. Udāsīna-vat, *as if* uninvolved: present, perceiving everything, contracted to nothing. Na iṅgate — the windless lamp of 6.19, returned.
verses.connection: The watcher's stance — 14.19's insight worn as a daily posture.
verses.reflection: Use the mantra at the next mood swing: gunah vartante — strands are turning — and do not stir.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the practical question — by what marks is the one beyond the strands known, and how does he cross them? Krishna gives the gunatita portrait: hating nothing that comes, craving nothing that ceases, seated as one unconcerned while the strands turn, equal in clod, stone, and gold — and then the method that overturns the chapter's analytics: unswerving devotion to Me carries one across, for I am the very foundation of Brahman, of immortality, and of absolute bliss.

verses.devanagari:
समदुःखसुखः स्वस्थः समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः ।
तुल्यप्रियाप्रियो धीरस्तुल्यनिन्दात्मसंस्तुतिः॥
verses.transliteration:
samaduḥkhasukhaḥ svasthaḥ samaloṣṭāśmakāñcanaḥ
tulyapriyāpriyo dhīrastulyanindātmasaṃstutiḥ
verses.essence: Alike in sorrow and joy, abiding in the self; alike toward a clod, a stone and gold; equal toward the dear and the undear; steady; equal in blame and in praise of oneself —
verses.translation: Who is the same in pleasure and pain, who dwells in the Self, to whom a clod of earth, stone and gold are alike, who is the same to the dear and the unfriendly, who is firm, and to whom censure and praise are as one.

anvaya.meaning: equal in sorrow-joy, abiding in oneself
alike toward dear and undear, steady
alike in blame and praise of oneself

verses.explanation: The equality-list returns from 6.8 and 12.18, with one word doing new work: sva-sthaḥ — *standing in one's own self*, self-stationed. That is the secret of all the samenesses that surround it: clod and gold weigh the same only to one whose footing depends on neither. The evenness is not effort; it is residence.
verses.connection: The portrait's center: every 'equal' in the list flows from one address — svastha.
verses.reflection: Stand in yourself once today — svastha — and let clod, stone, and gold briefly weigh the same.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the practical question — by what marks is the one beyond the strands known, and how does he cross them? Krishna gives the gunatita portrait: hating nothing that comes, craving nothing that ceases, seated as one unconcerned while the strands turn, equal in clod, stone, and gold — and then the method that overturns the chapter's analytics: unswerving devotion to Me carries one across, for I am the very foundation of Brahman, of immortality, and of absolute bliss.

verses.devanagari:
मानापमानयोस्तुल्यस्तुल्यो मित्रारिपक्षयोः ।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी गुणातीतः स उच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
mānāpamānayostulyastulyo mitrāripakṣayoḥ
sarvārambhaparityāgī guṇātītaḥ sa ucyate
verses.essence: — alike in honor and dishonor, alike toward the sides of friend and foe, renouncing all self-serving undertakings: that one is said to have gone beyond the gunas.
verses.translation: Who is the same in honour and dishonour, the same to friend and foe, abandoning all undertakings he is said to have transcended the alities.

anvaya.meaning: alike toward the sides of friend and enemy

verses.explanation: The portrait completes on Arjuna's exact wound: tulyo mitrāri-pakṣayoḥ — alike toward the *sides*, the pakṣa, friend's army and foe's. The man paralyzed in chapter 1 by which side held whom is shown a being for whom the sides have lost their pull. Guṇātītaḥ sa ucyate: the title conferred — and 14.26 will reveal the one practice that earns it.
verses.connection: Seals the portrait with the battlefield word 'paksha' — then the chapter springs its devotional turn.
verses.reflection: Feel the pull of the sides today — friend's and foe's — and practice one minute beyond both.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the practical question — by what marks is the one beyond the strands known, and how does he cross them? Krishna gives the gunatita portrait: hating nothing that comes, craving nothing that ceases, seated as one unconcerned while the strands turn, equal in clod, stone, and gold — and then the method that overturns the chapter's analytics: unswerving devotion to Me carries one across, for I am the very foundation of Brahman, of immortality, and of absolute bliss.

verses.devanagari:
मां च योऽव्यभिचारेण भक्तियोगेन सेवते ।
स गुणान्समतीत्यैतान्ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते॥
verses.transliteration:
māṃ ca yo'vyabhicāreṇa bhaktiyogena sevate
sa guṇānsamatītyaitānbrahmabhūyāya kalpate
verses.essence: And one who serves me with the unswerving yoga of devotion — that one, passing beyond these gunas, becomes fit for the state of Brahman.
verses.translation: And he who serves Me with unswerving devotion, he, crossing beyond the alities, is fit for becoming Brahman.

anvaya.meaning: that one, fully transcending these gunas
becomes fit for the state of Brahman

verses.explanation: Arjuna's third question — *how* does one cross? — answered at last, and the answer is not a technique of watching but a direction of loving: avyabhicāreṇa bhakti-yogena, devotion that does not stray. The logic is exact: every effort to climb out of the gunas is made *of* gunas; only attachment to what is beyond them can draw one beyond them. Love is the single rope let down from outside the weave. Mam cha yo 'vyabhicharena bhakti-yogena sevate — the verse where Arjuna's 'how does one cross?' is finally answered, and the answer reorganizes the chapter: not a technique against the gunas but a love beyond them. Sa gunan samatitya etan — fully transcending. The logic the teachers draw: every effort is made of strands; only attachment to the strand-free can pull one past the weave. Brahma-bhuyaya kalpate: devotion as the chemistry of becoming.
verses.connection: The chapter's resolution: the gunatita is made not by struggle against the strands but by devotion to their Lord.
verses.reflection: Use the one rope from outside the weave today: unswerving devotion — love your way past the strands.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the practical question — by what marks is the one beyond the strands known, and how does he cross them? Krishna gives the gunatita portrait: hating nothing that comes, craving nothing that ceases, seated as one unconcerned while the strands turn, equal in clod, stone, and gold — and then the method that overturns the chapter's analytics: unswerving devotion to Me carries one across, for I am the very foundation of Brahman, of immortality, and of absolute bliss.

verses.devanagari:
ब्रह्मणो हि प्रतिष्ठाहममृतस्याव्ययस्य च ।
शाश्वतस्य च धर्मस्य सुखस्यैकान्तिकस्य च॥
verses.transliteration:
brahmaṇo hi pratiṣṭhāhamamṛtasyāvyayasya ca
śāśvatasya ca dharmasya sukhasyaikāntikasya ca
verses.essence: For I am the foundation of Brahman — of the immortal and the imperishable, of the eternal dharma, and of absolute bliss.
verses.translation: For I am the abode of Brahman, the immortal and the immutable, of everlasting Dharma and of absolute bliss.

anvaya.meaning: for of Brahman, I am the foundation
of the immortal and the imperishable

verses.explanation: The chapter's last word inverts the expected hierarchy: brahmaṇo hi pratiṣṭhā aham — I am the *ground* on which Brahman stands. The impersonal Absolute, the immortal, dharma itself, final bliss: all listed, and the person speaking placed beneath them as their pedestal. Devotion to him, the verse implies, was never a stepping-stone to something higher — there is nothing higher to step to. Brahmano hi pratishthaham — for I am the foundation of Brahman: the chapter's last line and one of the tradition's great battlegrounds. Shankara reads the speaker as the unconditioned pointing through its own conditioned form; Ramanuja and the Gaudiyas read it flat: the personal Lord as the ground of the impersonal light, amritasya avyayasya cha — of the immortal, the imperishable, the eternal dharma, and absolute bliss. Either way the inversion stuns: Brahman has a pedestal, and it speaks.
verses.connection: Seals 14.26's claim with the chapter's boldest theology — and hands the personal-God thread to chapter 15.
verses.schools_note: A famous battleground. Advaita reads 'pratishtha' as Krishna speaking as the conditioned Brahman pointing to identity with the unconditioned. Ramanuja and the Vaishnava schools read it plainly: the personal Lord is the ground and fullness of the impersonal light — Brahman is his radiance, not his superior.
verses.reflection: Rest tonight on the foundation beneath even Brahman — the person this whole song is spoken by.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the practical question — by what marks is the one beyond the strands known, and how does he cross them? Krishna gives the gunatita portrait: hating nothing that comes, craving nothing that ceases, seated as one unconcerned while the strands turn, equal in clod, stone, and gold — and then the method that overturns the chapter's analytics: unswerving devotion to Me carries one across, for I am the very foundation of Brahman, of immortality, and of absolute bliss.
